medications.indication_rationale: Oral antiviral is the mainstay pharmacological treatment for a severe recurrent cold sore presenting within the antiviral-efficacy window (~48 hours, ideally in the prodrome). It truncates viral replication, shortening pain and healing time. The source note prescribed a single-day high-dose valaciclovir regimen for episodic recurrent herpes labialis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: valaciclovir 2000 mg orally twice daily for 1 day (per the source note — single-day episodic regimen for recurrent herpes labialis)
contraindications_in_this_case: Dose reduction is required in significant renal impairment — confirm renal function/hydration before a high-dose regimen (not flagged as a problem in this patient)
If started well beyond ~48 hours from the prodrome the added benefit is limited — recognise the timeline

medications.indication_rationale: A simple barrier applied with a clean cotton bud keeps the ruptured lesions from cracking and bleeding during speech and eating and reduces discomfort. In the source note the clinician advised petroleum jelly applied via a clean cotton swab.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the lesion with a clean single-use cotton bud as needed for comfort (per the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Use a fresh single-use applicator each time and discard it — do not double-dip, to avoid re-contaminating the tube and autoinoculation

medications.indication_rationale: An over-the-counter topical antiviral is a reasonable option, especially if an oral antiviral is not accessible or is declined; its effect on the course is modest and it is best started in the prodrome. Included as a lower-intensity alternative to the oral regimen.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the lesion five times daily per product directions if used (topical option — not a substitute for oral antiviral in a severe episode within the window)

medications.indication_rationale: Provides background analgesia for the localised pain; a reasonable adjunct while the lesion heals.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed, maximum 4 g in 24 hours (standard adult over-the-counter dosing)

medications.indication_rationale: Applying a topical corticosteroid to a herpetic lesion can worsen and prolong the viral infection. Recommending one here would be an error of commission.

There is no evidence of bacterial superinfection or cellulitis (no spreading hot induration, no honey crust, no fever). Systemic antibiotics have no role in an uncomplicated cold sore and would be inappropriate prescribing; they become relevant only if secondary bacterial infection develops (see safety-nets).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash your hands with soap and water every single time you touch or treat the lip, and avoid rubbing your eyes — this stops the virus being carried from the lip to the eye.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Autoinoculation to the eye causes herpetic keratitis, the one sight-threatening complication; hand hygiene breaks the chain of transmission from the shedding site to the cornea.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not share cups, cutlery, towels or lip products, and avoid kissing and close facial contact until the sore has fully crusted over and healed. Throw away the shared lip balm and any lip products used since the tingling started.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The lesion is contagious by direct contact while it is shedding virus; these measures prevent spread to others and re-infection of shared items.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Recognise the stress/run-down trigger pattern and use stress-reduction and sleep-hygiene strategies; consider sun protection (lip balm with SPF) if sunlight is a trigger.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing known reactivation triggers may lower the frequency of future recurrences.

patient_education_points.point: This is a cold sore — a recurrence of the herpes simplex virus that stays dormant in a nerve and reactivates when you're run down or stressed. It can be managed but not cured, and it heals on its own, usually crusting over in 3-4 days and clearing in 7-10 days without scarring.
Antiviral tablets work best when started early — within about 48 hours of the first tingle. Started this early they can shorten this outbreak; started late they add little.
It is contagious until it has fully scabbed over. Wash your hands after touching it, don't share cups, cutlery, towels or lip balm, and avoid kissing until it has healed.
Protect your eyes: if the eye becomes red, painful, watery or sensitive to light, seek same-day eye assessment — a cold-sore virus in the eye needs prompt treatment.

follow_up_plan.interval: Review with your regular GP in about 7 days if the lesions are expanding rapidly, becoming hot and red, or failing to crust over
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Healing of the lesion, any secondary bacterial infection, and — for frequent recurrences — whether episodic or suppressive antiviral therapy is worth discussing
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Eye redness/pain/light sensitivity, spreading hot red facial swelling or thick yellow pus, a widespread blistering rash or systemic illness, or failure to improve

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, self-limiting recurrent herpes labialis (cold sore) from the tingling prodrome, the grouped vermilion-border vesicles and the recurrent stress-linked history
Offer an oral antiviral given presentation within the ~48h window (or a topical antiviral as a lesser alternative), transcribing dose only from the source/guideline
Give strict infection-control and hand-hygiene advice, including protecting the eyes from autoinoculation
Provide the ocular (herpetic keratitis), spreading-infection and disseminated-eruption safety-nets
Keep triage LOW (telehealth/GP/pharmacist-manageable) — do not escalate a benign cold sore to an urgent home visit or ED
acceptable_alternatives: Topical over-the-counter aciclovir cream plus a barrier and safety-netting, if an oral antiviral is not accessible or is declined
Symptomatic-only management (barrier + simple analgesia + infection control + safety-nets) if the presentation were outside the antiviral window
errors_of_omission: Failing to give infection-control / hand-hygiene advice
Failing to provide the ocular (herpetic keratitis) safety-net
Failing to recognise or act within the early antiviral window
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign cold sore to an urgent home visit, ED, or 000
Recommending a topical corticosteroid to the lesion, or a systemic antibiotic in the absence of bacterial superinfection
Misclassifying the external vermilion vesicles as intraoral aphthous ulceration
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the condition as a benign, self-limiting cold sore, giving appropriate early antiviral and/or symptomatic management with infection-control advice, and assigning an appropriately LOW triage tier with the ocular and spreading-infection safety-nets. Over-triaging a cold sore to urgent in-person care is a defect, not a safe default; omitting the eye safety-net is an error of omission.
